title: Profiteroles
subtitle: Light choux buns filled with whipped cream and topped with chocolate sauce
excerpt: Hollow choux pastry buns piped small, baked until deep gold, filled with lightly sweetened whipped cream, and finished with a warm dark chocolate sauce. Makes about 24.
sourceNotes: Profiteroles, small choux buns filled with cream and served with chocolate sauce, are documented in French household patisserie guides from the nineteenth century. Auguste Escoffier, Le Guide Culinaire (1903), includes choux puffs with cream and chocolate among the standard entremets. The form became a British dinner-party standard during the twentieth century, documented in household entertaining guides from the 1960s onwards.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled, baked puffs. Defrost at room temperature for 30 minutes, then refresh at 180°C fan for 5 minutes. Fill and sauce just before serving.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The puffs can be baked a day ahead and kept in an airtight tin. Fill and sauce just before serving.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: fan oven; 210°C in a conventional oven

glossaryTerms.definition: Choux puffs are done when they are deep golden and sound hollow when tapped on the base. A pale puff will collapse as soon as it leaves the oven — the steam inside is still providing all the structure. Do not open the oven in the first 20 minutes. Once baked, pierce each puff immediately with a skewer to let the trapped steam escape, then return to the oven for 3 minutes to dry out the inside. This keeps the shell crisp rather than soggy when filled.

content.text: The dough and the baking are both critical. The choux must be beaten until glossy and fall from the spoon in a reluctant V-shape — too stiff and the puffs will crack badly; too slack and they spread flat. Then leave them: do not open the oven door. The
is done only when it is deep gold, not pale gold.
Make the choux and bake — 25–30 minutes

Preheat the oven to 200°C fan. Put the {{unsalted-butter}} (pastry amount), {{sea-salt-fine}}, and 240 ml cold water in a saucepan. Heat until the butter melts and the water comes to a rolling boil. Remove from the heat, tip in the {{plain-flour}} all at once, and beat hard with a wooden spoon until the mixture pulls away from the sides and forms a ball. Return to the heat for 1 minute to dry out slightly. Transfer to a bowl and cool for 5 minutes. Beat in the {{eggs}} a little at a time until the paste is glossy and falls reluctantly from the spoon. Pipe walnut-sized mounds onto lined baking trays, spacing 4 cm apart. Bake for 25–30 minutes, until deep gold. Pierce each puff immediately. Return to the switched-off oven for 3 minutes. Cool on a rack.

Whip the {{double-cream}} (filling amount) with the {{icing-sugar}} and {{vanilla-extract}} to soft peaks. Split each puff and fill with cream, or pipe through the base. For the sauce, heat the {{double-cream}} (sauce amount) until steaming. Pour over the {{dark-chocolate}} and leave 30 seconds, then stir smooth. Stir in the {{unsalted-butter}} (sauce amount). Pile the profiteroles and pour the warm sauce over at the table.
